title: Lion of Judah

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Lion+of+Judah%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Lion+of+Judah%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Lion+of+Judah%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Lion+of+Judah%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(May 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): אריה יהודה, *Aryeh Yehudah*) is a [Jewish national and cultural symbol](/wiki/Jewish_symbolism "Jewish symbolism"), traditionally regarded as the symbol of the [tribe of Judah](/wiki/Tribe_of_Judah "Tribe of Judah"). The association between the Judahites and the [lion](/wiki/Asiatic_lion "Asiatic lion") can first be found in the blessing given by [Jacob](/wiki/Jacob "Jacob") to his fourth son, [Judah](/wiki/Judah_(son_of_Jacob) "Judah (son of Jacob)"), in the [Book of Genesis](/wiki/Book_of_Genesis "Book of Genesis") of the [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

["Genesis 49:9"](http://biblehub.com/genesis/49-9.htm). Biblos. [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20150217060800/http://biblehub.com/genesis/49-9.htm) from the original on 17 February 2015. Retrieved 1 December 2014.

["Revelation 5:5"](http://www.biblestudytools.com/commentaries/revelation/revelation-5/revelation-5-5.html). Bible Study Tools. [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20150119173145/http://www.biblestudytools.com/commentaries/revelation/revelation-5/revelation-5-5.html) from the original on 19 January 2015. Retrieved 1 December 2014.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Jewish national and cultural symbol

For the animated film, see [The Lion of Judah](/wiki/The_Lion_of_Judah "The Lion of Judah").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Lion_of_Judah "Special:EditPage/Lion of Judah") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Lion of Judah"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Lion+of+Judah%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Lion+of+Judah%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Lion+of+Judah%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Lion+of+Judah%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Lion+of+Judah%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Lion+of+Judah%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(May 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/%D7%A9%D7%90%D7%92%D7%AA_%D7%94%D7%90%D7%A8%D7%99%D7%94.jpg/250px-%D7%A9%D7%90%D7%92%D7%AA_%D7%94%D7%90%D7%A8%D7%99%D7%94.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D7%A9%D7%90%D7%92%D7%AA_%D7%94%D7%90%D7%A8%D7%99%D7%94.jpg)

*[The Roaring Lion](/wiki/The_Roaring_Lion_(sculpture) "The Roaring Lion (sculpture)")* sculpture in Israel

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Mosav_zkenim_003.jpg/250px-Mosav_zkenim_003.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mosav_zkenim_003.jpg)

The Lion of Judah on a Bezalel ceramic tile

The **Lion of Judah** ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): אריה יהודה, *Aryeh Yehudah*) is a [Jewish national and cultural symbol](/wiki/Jewish_symbolism "Jewish symbolism"), traditionally regarded as the symbol of the [tribe of Judah](/wiki/Tribe_of_Judah "Tribe of Judah"). The association between the Judahites and the [lion](/wiki/Asiatic_lion "Asiatic lion") can first be found in the blessing given by [Jacob](/wiki/Jacob "Jacob") to his fourth son, [Judah](/wiki/Judah_(son_of_Jacob) "Judah (son of Jacob)"), in the [Book of Genesis](/wiki/Book_of_Genesis "Book of Genesis") of the [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

It is also mentioned in the [Book of Revelation](/wiki/Book_of_Revelation "Book of Revelation") of the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") as a term representing [Jesus of Nazareth](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus"), according to [Christian theology](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The Lion of Judah was also one of the titles used by [Ethiopian emperors](/wiki/Emperor_of_Ethiopia "Emperor of Ethiopia") from the [Solomonic dynasty](/wiki/Solomonic_dynasty "Solomonic dynasty").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lion_of_Judah&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Judaism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lion_of_Judah&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Judaism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Emblem_of_Jerusalem.svg/250px-Emblem_of_Jerusalem.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Emblem_of_Jerusalem.svg)

[Emblem of Jerusalem](/wiki/Emblem_of_Jerusalem "Emblem of Jerusalem")

The biblical [Judah](/wiki/Judah_(Biblical_figure) "Judah (Biblical figure)") (in [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): *Yehuda*) is the [eponymous](/wiki/Eponym "Eponym") ancestor of the [Tribe of Judah](/wiki/Tribe_of_Judah "Tribe of Judah"), which is traditionally symbolized by a lion. In [Genesis](/wiki/Book_of_Genesis "Book of Genesis"), the patriarch [Jacob](/wiki/Jacob "Jacob") ("Israel") gave that symbol to this tribe when he refers to his son Judah as a *Gur Aryeh'* גּוּר אַרְיֵה יְהוּדָה, "Young Lion" ([Genesis 49](/wiki/Genesis_49 "Genesis 49"):9) when blessing him.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In [Jewish](/wiki/Jew "Jew") naming tradition the Hebrew name and the substitute name are often combined as a pair, as in this case. The Lion of Judah was used as a Jewish symbol for many years, and as [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") was the capital of the [Kingdom of Judah](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Judah "Kingdom of Judah"), in 1950 it was included in the [Emblem of Jerusalem](/wiki/Emblem_of_Jerusalem "Emblem of Jerusalem").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lion_of_Judah&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Christianity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Thesouro_de_Nobreza._Judah.png/250px-Thesouro_de_Nobreza._Judah.png)](/wiki/File:Thesouro_de_Nobreza._Judah.png)

Lion of Judah ("Thesouro de Nobreza", 1675)

The phrase appears in the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") in [Revelation 5:5](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Revelation%205:5&version=nrsv):

> Then one of the elders said to me, "Do not weep. See, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered, so that he can open the scroll and its seven seals."

This is widely regarded as a reference to the [Second Coming](/wiki/Second_Coming "Second Coming") among [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Many Christian organizations and ministries use the lion of Judah as their emblem or even as their names.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Ethiopia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lion_of_Judah&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ethiopia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Ethiopian_imperial_standard_of_Haile_Selassie_I_%28obverse%29.svg/250px-Ethiopian_imperial_standard_of_Haile_Selassie_I_%28obverse%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ethiopian_imperial_standard_of_Haile_Selassie_I_(obverse).svg)

Imperial Flag of Haile Selassie of Ethiopia with the Lion of Judah, cross scepter, five [Stars of David](/wiki/Star_of_David "Star of David") and the words "Conquering Lion of the Tribe of Judah" in Amharic

It was depicted on a map of the Upper Nile published in 1683 by the German [Hiob Ludolf](/wiki/Hiob_Ludolf "Hiob Ludolf") describing the Lion of Judah symbol as the [royal insignia](/wiki/Royal_insignia "Royal insignia") of the [Ethiopian Empire](/wiki/Ethiopian_Empire "Ethiopian Empire"). The [Solomonic dynasty](/wiki/Solomonic_dynasty "Solomonic dynasty") of Ethiopia claims to have its patrilineal origin in the Israelite Royal House of Judah. The Lion of Judah served as the hereditary title of the Solomonic [Ethiopian emperors](/wiki/Emperor_of_Ethiopia "Emperor of Ethiopia") and nobles including [Sabagadis Woldu](/wiki/Sabagadis_Woldu "Sabagadis Woldu"), [Kenfu Hailu](/wiki/Kenfu_Hailu "Kenfu Hailu"), [Wube Haile Mariam](/wiki/Wube_Haile_Mariam "Wube Haile Mariam"), [Tewodros II](/wiki/Tewodros_II "Tewodros II"), [Yohannes IV](/wiki/Yohannes_IV "Yohannes IV"), [Menelik](/wiki/Menelik_II "Menelik II"), and [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") and was depicted on the [flag of Ethiopia](/wiki/Flag_of_Ethiopia "Flag of Ethiopia") from 1897 to 1974. Due to its association with Haile Selassie, it continues to be an important symbol among members of the [Rastafari movement](/wiki/Rastafari_movement "Rastafari movement").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The Lion of Judah motif figured prominently on the old imperial flag, currency, stamps, etc. and may still be seen gracing the terrace of the capital as a national symbol. After the collapse of the [Derg](/wiki/Derg "Derg") in 1990, a minor political party bearing the name *Mo'a Anbessa'* made its appearance.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### *Kebra Nagast*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lion_of_Judah&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Kebra Nagast")]

[Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia")'s history as recorded and elaborated in a 13th-century treatise, the *[Kebra Nagast](/wiki/Kebra_Nagast "Kebra Nagast")*, asserts descent from a retinue of Israelites who returned with Makeda, the [Queen of Sheba](/wiki/Queen_of_Sheba "Queen of Sheba") from her visit to [King Solomon](/wiki/King_Solomon "King Solomon") in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), by whom she had conceived the Solomonic dynasty's founder [Menelik I](/wiki/Menelik_I "Menelik I"). As Solomon was of the tribe of Judah, his son Menelik I would continue the line, which according to Ethiopian history was passed directly down from king to king until Emperor [Haile Selassie I](/wiki/Haile_Selassie_I "Haile Selassie I") (ostensibly the 225th king from [King David](/wiki/King_David "King David")) was deposed in 1974.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Both [Christian](/wiki/Ethiopian_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church") and [Jewish Ethiopian](/wiki/Beta_Israel "Beta Israel") history have it that there were also immigrants of the Tribes of [Dan](/wiki/Tribe_of_Dan "Tribe of Dan") and Judah that accompanied Makeda back from her visit to Solomon; hence the [Ge'ez](/wiki/Ge%27ez_language "Ge'ez language") motto *Mo`a 'Anbessa Ze'imnegede Yihuda* ("The Lion of Judah has conquered"), included among the titles of the Emperor throughout the Solomonic Dynasty.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Rastafari

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lion_of_Judah&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Rastafari")]

The Lion of Judah is a prominent symbol in the [Rastafari movement](/wiki/Rastafari_movement "Rastafari movement"). It represents [Emperor Haile Selassie I](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") as well as being a symbol of strength, kingship, pride and African sovereignty.[[10]](#cite_note-Barnett_2014-10)
Rastafari consider the mention of "The Lion of Judah" in [Genesis 49](/wiki/Genesis_49 "Genesis 49"):9 and [Revelation 5](/wiki/Revelation_5 "Revelation 5"):5 of the Bible to refer to [Emperor Haile Selassie I](/wiki/Emperor_Haile_Selassie_I "Emperor Haile Selassie I"). Rastafari hail Haile Selassie I with the titles "King of kings, lord of lords, conquering Lion of Judah, elect of God, the light of the world".[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## In literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lion_of_Judah&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: In literature")]

Inspired by the Lion of Judah, [C. S. Lewis](/wiki/C._S._Lewis "C. S. Lewis") used a lion named [Aslan](/wiki/Aslan "Aslan") to represent [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") in *[The Chronicles of Narnia](/wiki/The_Chronicles_of_Narnia "The Chronicles of Narnia")*.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lion_of_Judah&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Monument to the Lion of Judah](/wiki/Monument_to_the_Lion_of_Judah "Monument to the Lion of Judah"), a historic statue in Ethiopia
